content: The next Georgia primary election in Macon-Bibb County is Tuesday, May 19, 2026. Polls are open from 7:00 AM to 7:00 PM at each voter's assigned Election Day precinct. The primary includes partisan primaries for statewide, congressional, and state legislative offices, as well as nonpartisan judicial and local contests.

content: At the May 19, 2026 Georgia primary, Macon-Bibb County voters must choose either a Democratic, Republican, or nonpartisan-only ballot. The choice of party applies only to partisan primary races; nonpartisan races such as judicial and school board contests appear on all ballots regardless of which party ballot is selected. Georgia has no party registration, so any registered voter may request any party's primary ballot.
hypothetical_questions: Do I have to pick a party to vote in the primary?

How does Georgia's open primary work?

content: If no candidate in any Macon-Bibb County race receives more than 50% of the vote in the May 19, 2026 primary, a primary runoff will be held on June 16, 2026. Voters who were registered by the April 20, 2026 primary deadline are already registered for the runoff and do not need to re-register. The general election follows on November 3, 2026.

content: The Georgia governor's race is the top contest on the May 19, 2026 primary ballot. It is an open seat with no incumbent running, and both Democratic and Republican primaries are heavily contested. The governor serves a four-year term as Georgia's chief executive and sets policy that directly affects Macon-Bibb County services, education, and infrastructure.

content: Seven Democrats qualified for the May 19, 2026 governor primary: Amanda Duffy, Derrick Jackson, Geoff Duncan, Jason Esteves, Keisha Lance Bottoms, Michael Thurmond, and Olu Brown. Notable figures include Keisha Lance Bottoms (former Atlanta mayor), Michael Thurmond (DeKalb County CEO), Geoff Duncan (former Republican lieutenant governor), and Jason Esteves (state senator). A runoff will occur on June 16 if no Democrat clears 50%.

content: Bibb County is split between two U.S. House districts following the court-ordered 2023 redistricting: the 2nd Congressional District and the 12th Congressional District. Which district a Macon-Bibb voter is in depends on their specific address within the county. Voters can verify their assigned district at mvp.sos.ga.gov.

content: Bibb County school board members set policy and approve budgets for the Bibb County School District, which serves approximately 21,000 students across 35 or more schools. Board members serve four-year terms in nonpartisan elections, with at-large posts elected countywide. The district superintendent is Dr. Dan A. Sims, who has served since July 2022.

content: Four Macon Water Authority races appear on the May 19, 2026 nonpartisan ballot: Chairman (countywide), a special election for District 2, District 4, and District 1. The Macon Water Authority is a seven-member board with five single-member district seats and two countywide seats that oversees drinking water, wastewater, and stormwater systems for Macon-Bibb County. These races directly affect water quality and utility rates for every household.

content: The Macon Water Authority District 2 seat is a special election on the May 19, 2026 ballot because incumbent Desmond Brown resigned from the seat on March 6, 2026 to qualify for the Chairman's race. Under the authority's charter, when a vacancy will last more than 120 days, it must be filled at the next general election rather than by appointment. This special election was added to the ballot on March 20, 2026 after Chairman Gary Bechtel requested the Board of Elections include it.
hypothetical_questions: Why is there a Water Authority special election?

content: Four candidates are running in the May 19, 2026 special election for Macon Water Authority District 2: Marshall Talley (hospitality business owner), Ronald Edward Lemon (chiropractor), Sharif Robbins-Brinson (public administration graduate student and local Democratic Party officer), and Renoalda Scott (property manager, tax preparer, and chair of the Macon-Bibb County Democratic Committee). District 2 covers parts of East Macon west of Gray Highway, downtown, Pleasant Hill, Vineville, Ingleside, Unionville, Payne City, Napier Heights, Bloomfield, and south Bibb County from Eisenhower Parkway to Rocky Creek Road. The district has approximately 29,000 registered voters.

content: The Macon Water Authority Chairman's race on the May 19, 2026 ballot is a countywide nonpartisan contest between incumbent Gary Bechtel and challenger Desmond Brown. Bechtel, 65, was appointed to the board in 2017-18 and elected chair in 2022, defeating Brown in a June 2022 runoff by 8,519 to 3,826 votes. A third candidate, Donald Druitt, qualified but has dropped out of the race.

content: Three candidates are running for Macon Water Authority District 4 on May 19, 2026: 22-year incumbent Frank Patterson (age 78, retired from Bibb County public works and engineering), De'Ron Rogers (age 62, owner of Pinky's Shaved Ice and a 2024 candidate for State House District 145), and Michael McKeever (age 32, software program manager at Robins Air Force Base). District 4 covers southwest Bibb County, Lizella, Lake Tobesofkee, the Macon Mall, Rutland, and a slice of north Macon, with approximately 29,000 registered voters. The board is currently seeking a new CEO following the February 2026 death of longtime CEO Ron Shipman.

content: The voter registration deadline for the May 19, 2026 Georgia primary is Monday, April 20, 2026. Georgia residents can register online at registertovote.sos.ga.gov using a Georgia driver's license or state ID, by mail with an application from the Secretary of State's website, or in person at the Bibb County Board of Elections at 3661 Eisenhower Parkway, Suite MB101, Macon, GA 31206. Eligibility requires U.S. citizenship, residency in Georgia and Bibb County, and being at least 18 years old by Election Day.
hypothetical_questions: When is the voter registration deadline?
How do I register to vote in Macon?

Where do I register to vote in Bibb County?

content: Early voting for the May 19, 2026 Macon-Bibb County primary runs Monday, April 27, 2026 through Friday, May 15, 2026. Georgia law requires Saturday early voting on May 2 and May 9, 2026. Optional Sunday voting on May 3 and May 10 is at the county's discretion. During early voting, any registered Macon-Bibb County voter may vote at any open early voting location in the county.

content: The primary early voting location in Macon-Bibb County is the Board of Elections office at 3661 Eisenhower Parkway, Suite MB101, Macon, GA 31206, inside the Macon Mall. Weekday early voting hours are typically 8:30 AM to 5:30 PM, Saturday hours are typically 9:00 AM to 5:00 PM, and Sunday hours when offered are typically 1:00 PM to 5:00 PM. Additional satellite locations are likely for this major statewide primary; voters should confirm exact locations and hours at maconbibb.us/board-of-elections or (478) 621-6622.

content: The last day to request an absentee ballot for the May 19, 2026 primary is Friday, May 8, 2026. Georgia voters do not need an excuse to vote absentee. Requests can be made online at securemyabsenteeballot.sos.ga.gov, by mail, by fax to (478) 621-6119, by email to boe@maconbibb.us, or in person at the Board of Elections office. Voters must specify Democratic, Republican, or nonpartisan ballot and provide their Georgia driver's license or state ID number.
hypothetical_questions: When is the absentee ballot deadline?
How do I request an absentee ballot?

What is the last day to request an absentee ballot?

content: Completed absentee ballots must be received by the Bibb County Board of Elections no later than 7:00 PM on Election Day, May 19, 2026. Ballots can be returned by U.S. mail, hand-delivered to the Board of Elections office at 3661 Eisenhower Parkway during business hours, or deposited in the secure drop box inside the office during advance voting hours. Voters can track absentee ballot status at mvp.sos.ga.gov.
hypothetical_questions: When is the absentee ballot due?

content: On Election Day, May 19, 2026, Macon-Bibb County voters must vote at their assigned precinct polling location. Unlike early voting, Election Day voting is restricted to the voter's specific precinct. Voters can find their assigned polling place at mvp.sos.ga.gov, at maconbibb.us/board-of-elections/where-to-vote, or by calling (478) 621-6622.

content: The Bibb County Board of Elections is located at 3661 Eisenhower Parkway, Suite MB101, Macon, GA 31206, inside the Macon Mall. The office can be reached at (478) 621-6622, fax (478) 621-6119, or email boe@maconbibb.us, open Monday through Friday 8:30 AM to 5:30 PM. The elections supervisor is Tom Gillon (tgillon@maconbibb.us), and the website is maconbibb.us/board-of-elections.

content: To check voter registration, find a polling location, or view a personalized sample ballot for the May 19, 2026 primary, voters can visit the Georgia Secretary of State's My Voter Page at mvp.sos.ga.gov. The tool shows assigned congressional, state legislative, and county commission districts and displays only the races on that specific voter's ballot. The Macon-Bibb Board of Elections also posts a candidate list PDF at maconbibb.us/board-of-elections.

content: Key dates for the May 19, 2026 primary in Macon-Bibb County: voter registration deadline April 20, early voting April 27 through May 15, absentee ballot request deadline May 8, Election Day May 19 with polls open 7:00 AM to 7:00 PM. content: Lester Miller is the current Mayor of Macon-Bibb County, serving his second and final four-year term. He was first elected in 2019, took office January 2020, and was re-elected in May 2024. His current term runs through January 2029. Miller is the second mayor of the consolidated government, succeeding Robert Reichert, who was elected the first mayor in September 2013.

content: Commissioner Valerie Wynn of District 1 serves as the current Mayor Pro Tem of Macon-Bibb County. She was elected to the role by a 5-3 commission vote on January 6, 2026, replacing Seth Clark, who resigned from both his District 5 seat and the Mayor Pro Tem position on January 5, 2026 to run for lieutenant governor. The Mayor Pro Tem presides over commission meetings in the mayor's absence and performs the mayor's duties when the mayor cannot serve.

content: The 2024 Macon-Bibb County elections produced significant turnover on the commission: four of nine seats changed hands because longtime commissioners were term-limited. Elaine Lucas (District 3), Mallory Jones (District 4), Virgil Watkins (District 8), and Al Tillman (District 9) all reached their term limits. New commissioners Stanley Stewart, Joey Hulett, Donice Bryant, and Brendalyn Bailey replaced them.

content: Dr. Keith Moffett serves as Macon-Bibb County Manager, overseeing the day-to-day administration of 21 county departments with an approximate annual budget of $550 million. He was unanimously appointed by the commission in 2018 and is responsible for implementing the policies and directives of the mayor and commission. Julie Moore serves as assistant county manager.

content: Under Section 5 of the Macon-Bibb County Charter, the governing authority consists of a commission composed of a mayor and nine commissioners, designated as the Macon-Bibb County Commission. The commission constitutes both a county and a municipality for purposes of applying Georgia's general laws and Constitution. It exercises the powers vested in both municipal and county governments.
hypothetical_questions: How is the Macon-Bibb government structured?
How many commissioners are there?
What is the Macon-Bibb County Commission?
Is Macon a city or a county?

content: Under Section 9 of the Macon-Bibb County Charter, the county is divided into nine election districts designated Commission Districts 1 through 9. Each of the nine commissioners is elected from and must reside in one of these single-member districts. Initial district boundaries were set by a districting plan attached to the charter and based on the 2010 United States decennial census.
hypothetical_questions: How many commission districts are there?
How are commissioners elected?
What are the commission districts?
Are commissioners elected countywide?

content: Under Section 14 of the Macon-Bibb County Charter, all executive powers of the restructured government are vested in the mayor, who serves as chief executive officer. The mayor is responsible for executing the laws and policies of the government, supervising executive and administrative affairs, and representing the government in intergovernmental relations. The mayor also submits the annual budget, proposes ordinances, and signs contracts.
hypothetical_questions: What does the mayor do?
What are the mayor's powers?
Is the mayor the chief executive?
What authority does the mayor have?

content: Under Section 9(c) of the Macon-Bibb County Charter, the mayor is the presiding officer of the commission but is not a voting member. However, the mayor may cast a vote on any matter before the commission to break a tie. The mayor may propose ordinances in the same manner as a commissioner.

Does the mayor break ties?
Is the mayor part of the commission?

content: Under Section 10(e) of the Macon-Bibb County Charter, the mayor is limited to serving no more than two four-year terms. An initial term of less than four years still counts as a full term toward this limit. The mayor is elected countywide by majority vote in a nonpartisan election.
hypothetical_questions: How long is the mayor's term?
Can the mayor serve three terms?
What are the mayor's term limits?
How many terms can the mayor serve?

content: Under Section 10(c) of the Macon-Bibb County Charter, to be eligible to serve as mayor a person must have been a resident of Macon-Bibb County for one year before the election, continue to reside in the county during the term, be a registered and qualified elector of Macon-Bibb County, be at least 27 years old at the time of election, and be a U.S. citizen who has been a Georgia citizen for at least two years. These are the minimum constitutional qualifications for the office.
hypothetical_questions: Who can run for mayor?
What are the requirements to be mayor?
How old do you have to be to run for mayor?
What are the eligibility requirements for mayor?

content: Under Section 14(c) of the Macon-Bibb County Charter, the mayor serves full time and receives an annual salary of $100,000 plus reimbursement for actual official expenses. Any future increase in the mayor's salary is limited to no more than one-half of the average percentage salary increase granted to government employees over the preceding three years. The mayor is also treated as a full-time employee for benefits eligibility.
hypothetical_questions: How much does the mayor make?
What is the mayor's salary?
Is the mayor a full-time job?

content: Under Section 10(e) of the Macon-Bibb County Charter, each commissioner is limited to serving a total of three four-year terms. Terms begin on the first day of January following the nonpartisan election and last four years. An initial term of less than four years still counts as a full term toward the limit.
hypothetical_questions: How long is a commissioner's term?
Can a commissioner serve four terms?
What are the term limits for commissioners?
How many terms can a commissioner serve?

content: Under Section 9(d) of the Macon-Bibb County Charter, to be eligible to serve as a commissioner a person must have been a resident of Macon-Bibb County for one year before the election, continue to reside in the commission district from which elected throughout the term, be a registered qualified elector, be at least 21 years old, be a U.S. citizen, and have been a Georgia citizen for at least two years. Commissioners must also have been a legal resident of their specific district for at least one year before election (with narrow exceptions for newly drawn districts).
hypothetical_questions: Who can run for commissioner?
What are the requirements to be a commissioner?
How old do you have to be to run for commission?
Do commissioners have to live in their district?

content: Under Section 9(e) of the Macon-Bibb County Charter, each commissioner is paid an annual salary of $15,000. Any salary increase is limited to no more than one-half of the average percentage salary increase granted to government employees over the preceding three years, and takes effect only at the start of a new term following the next regular election. Commissioners are treated as part-time employees for benefits purposes.
hypothetical_questions: How much does a commissioner make?
What is a commissioner's salary?
Are commissioners full-time?

content: Under Section 9(f) of the Macon-Bibb County Charter, the commission elects one of its own members to serve as Mayor Pro Tempore each January. The Mayor Pro Tem presides over commission meetings in the mayor's absence. The selection happens annually among the nine commissioners.
hypothetical_questions: How is the Mayor Pro Tem chosen?
When is the Mayor Pro Tem elected?
Who picks the Mayor Pro Tem?

content: Under Section 19(a) of the Macon-Bibb County Charter, six members of the commission constitute a quorum for the transaction of ordinary business. A quorum is the minimum number of commissioners who must be present for the commission to legally conduct business at a meeting.
hypothetical_questions: What is a quorum for the commission?
How many commissioners make a quorum?
What is the quorum?
How many commissioners need to be present?

content: Under Section 19(a) of the Macon-Bibb County Charter, the affirmative vote of at least five members is required for the commission to take official action. This is the default voting threshold for passing ordinances and resolutions — a simple majority of the nine-member commission. Any member may request a roll-call vote, and official actions are entered upon the commission's minutes.
hypothetical_questions: How does the commission vote?
How many votes are needed to pass an ordinance?
What vote does the commission need?
What is the voting threshold on the commission?
How many commissioners need to vote yes?

content: Under Section 14(b) of the Macon-Bibb County Charter, every ordinance adopted by the commission is presented to the mayor, who has ten calendar days to approve, disapprove (veto), or take no action. If the mayor approves, the ordinance becomes law immediately. If the mayor takes no action, the ordinance automatically becomes law at 12:00 Noon on the tenth calendar day. If the mayor vetoes, the ordinance is returned with a written statement of reasons and does not take effect unless overridden.
hypothetical_questions: Can the mayor veto an ordinance?
What is the mayor's veto power?
How long does the mayor have to veto?
What happens if the mayor doesn't sign an ordinance?

content: Under Section 14(b)(3) of the Macon-Bibb County Charter, a mayoral veto can be overridden by the affirmative vote of at least six members of the commission. The vetoed ordinance is presented to the commissioners at their next meeting, and they may override the veto at that meeting or the one after. If the commission musters six or more yes votes, the ordinance becomes law despite the mayor's objection.
hypothetical_questions: Can the commission override a veto?
How many votes are needed to override a veto?
What is a veto override?
How does the commission override the mayor?

content: Under Section 14(b)(4) of the Macon-Bibb County Charter, the mayor may disapprove or reduce any individual item of appropriation in an ordinance — a line-item veto. The approved portions of the appropriations ordinance become law, while disapproved or reduced items do not become law unless the commission overrides with at least six affirmative votes. This gives the mayor targeted authority over spending decisions.
hypothetical_questions: Does the mayor have a line-item veto?
Can the mayor veto part of a budget?
Can the mayor reduce appropriations?

content: Under Section 19(b) of the Macon-Bibb County Charter, the commission must hold at least one regular meeting each month. The date and time of regular meetings are set by the commission's own rules of procedure. In practice, regular commission meetings are held on the first and third Tuesdays of each month at 6:00 PM.
hypothetical_questions: How often does the commission meet?
When are commission meetings?
What day does the commission meet?
When is the next commission meeting?
source: Macon-Bibb County Charter, Section 19(b); Macon-Bibb County government

content: Under Section 19(c) of the Macon-Bibb County Charter, special commission meetings may be called by the Mayor Pro Tem or a majority of all commission members. The mayor may also call special meetings for fiscal affairs or emergencies involving public safety. Notice must be served on commissioners at least 48 hours in advance (or 24 hours if delivered by law enforcement), and only the business stated in the call may be transacted.
hypothetical_questions: Who can call a special commission meeting?
What is a special meeting?
How much notice is required for a special meeting?

content: Under Section 19(d) of the Macon-Bibb County Charter and Georgia's Open Meetings Act (O.C.G.A. Chapter 14 of Title 50), all commission meetings are public. Notice to the public of special meetings must be provided as fully as reasonably possible at least 48 hours before the meeting. Meetings are also livestreamed on the county's Facebook page and archived at maconbibb.tv.
hypothetical_questions: Are commission meetings public?
Can I watch a commission meeting?
Are meetings livestreamed?
Is there public notice for meetings?

content: Under Section 19(e) of the Macon-Bibb County Charter, the commission may adopt emergency ordinances to meet public emergencies affecting life, health, property, or public peace. Emergency ordinances cannot levy taxes, grant franchises, set utility rates, or borrow money (except for loans repayable within 30 days). An emergency ordinance must be plainly designated as such and contain a declaration describing the emergency in specific terms.
hypothetical_questions: Can the commission pass emergency ordinances?
What is an emergency ordinance?
What can't emergency ordinances do?

content: Under Section 15(a) of the Macon-Bibb County Charter, if the office of mayor or any commissioner becomes vacant, the commission (or those remaining) must order a special election by resolution to fill the balance of the unexpired term. This is the default rule for filling mid-term vacancies.
hypothetical_questions: What happens if a commissioner resigns?
How are vacancies filled?
What if the mayor resigns?
How do special elections work for commission seats?

content: Under Section 15(b) of the Macon-Bibb County Charter, if a commission seat becomes vacant within 12 months of the end of the term, the remaining commissioners may appoint a successor for the remainder of the term within 20 days of the vacancy rather than holding a special election. This exception avoids costly special elections for short remainders.
hypothetical_questions: What if a vacancy happens near the end of a term?
Can the commission appoint someone to fill a seat?
When can vacancies be filled by appointment?

content: Under Section 15(c) of the Macon-Bibb County Charter, if the office of mayor becomes vacant, the Mayor Pro Tem serves as acting mayor with all the powers of that office until a successor is elected and qualified. If the mayoral vacancy occurs within 12 months of the term's end, the Mayor Pro Tem succeeds to the office of mayor for the remainder of the term, and their commission seat is then filled under the standard vacancy rules.
hypothetical_questions: What happens if the mayor resigns?
Who becomes mayor if there's a vacancy?
Does the Mayor Pro Tem become mayor?

content: Under Section 18 of the Macon-Bibb County Charter, no person can be appointed to fill a vacancy for an unexpired term unless that person receives a majority of the votes of all commissioners then serving. This threshold is based on the number actually serving, not the full nine-member count.
hypothetical_questions: How many votes are needed to appoint a replacement?
What is the appointment threshold?
How are vacancy appointments approved?

content: Under Section 11 of the Macon-Bibb County Charter, the commission has broad legislative powers including: adopting the annual budget by ordinance, setting the fiscal year, establishing or abolishing departments, adopting personnel policies, adjusting district boundaries, establishing committees, granting franchises and regulating public utilities, and collecting residential solid waste. The commission may also adopt any other ordinance or resolution allowed by state law or the charter.
hypothetical_questions: What powers does the commission have?
What can the commission do?
Can the commission set the budget?
Can the commission create departments?

content: Under Section 16(a)(1) of the Macon-Bibb County Charter, the mayor appoints a county manager, subject to approval by a majority of the commission, who serves at the pleasure of the mayor. The county manager must hold either a master's degree plus 4 years of experience, or a bachelor's degree plus 8 years, in public or business administration. The county manager directs the department of administration and coordinates activities of all county departments.
hypothetical_questions: How is the county manager appointed?
What are the county manager qualifications?
Does the county manager need a degree?
What does the county manager do?

content: Under Section 16 of the Macon-Bibb County Charter, the mayor appoints several key officials subject to majority approval by the commission, all serving at the pleasure of the mayor: the county manager, the clerk of commission, the fire chief, the county attorney, the municipal county judge, and department heads generally. The clerk of commission is specifically approved by majority vote of all commissioners then serving rather than just those present.
hypothetical_questions: Who does the mayor appoint?
How are department heads chosen?
Does the commission approve mayor's appointments?
Who appoints the fire chief?

content: Under Section 12 of the Macon-Bibb County Charter, the commission must provide for an independent annual audit of all government accounts, initiated with the approval of either the mayor and five commissioners OR six commissioners acting alone. Audits must be performed by a certified public accountant with no personal interest in the government's finances. The commission may also order special independent audits of any office or agency without the mayor's approval.
hypothetical_questions: Does Macon-Bibb have an annual audit?
How does the audit work?
Who conducts the audit?
Can the commission order an audit?

content: Under Section 13 of the Macon-Bibb County Charter, the commission may initiate inquiries and investigations into the affairs of the government and the conduct of any department, office, agency, or joint commission, with the approval of either the mayor and five commissioners OR six commissioners acting alone. All such hearings are subject to Georgia's Open Meetings Act, and any action taken as a result must occur during an open meeting.
hypothetical_questions: Can the commission investigate departments?
What investigative powers does the commission have?
Can the commission hold hearings?

content: Under Section 26(a) of the Macon-Bibb County Charter, the mayor must submit to the commission at least six weeks before the start of each fiscal year a budget message, budget report, and draft appropriations ordinance. The commission reviews and adopts the final budget by ordinance before the fiscal year begins. This gives the commission roughly six weeks to review and modify the mayor's proposal.
hypothetical_questions: When is the budget submitted?
How does the budget process work?
Who prepares the budget?
When does the commission adopt the budget?

content: Under Section 35 of the Macon-Bibb County Charter, the charter may be modified, rescinded, changed, or amended only by two methods: an Act of the Georgia General Assembly, or an ordinance adopted by the commission. This limited set of amendment methods makes the charter relatively stable compared to ordinary ordinances.
hypothetical_questions: How is the charter amended?
Can the charter be changed?
Who changes the charter?

content: Macon-Bibb County, Georgia had a population of 157,346 in the 2020 U.S. Census. The most recent Census Bureau estimate places the figure at approximately 157,000. The county covers 255 square miles, and its demographics are approximately 54% Black or African American, 36% White, 4% Hispanic or Latino, and 2% Asian.
hypothetical_questions: What is the population of Macon?
How many people live in Macon-Bibb?
How big is Macon-Bibb County?
What are Macon's demographics?

content: Macon and Bibb County consolidated into a single unified government on January 1, 2014, after voters approved the merger in a July 31, 2012 referendum. Macon city voters approved with 57.8% and Bibb County voters with 56.7%. The consolidation merged the City of Macon, the City of Payne City, and Bibb County into one government called Macon-Bibb County. Four previous consolidation attempts had failed — in 1933, 1960, 1972, and 1976 — making the 2012 vote a historic turning point for Middle Georgia.
hypothetical_questions: When did Macon and Bibb County merge?
When did consolidation happen?
Why did Macon and Bibb County consolidate?
What was the 2012 referendum?

content: The consolidation of Macon and Bibb County means that one government provides both city and county services, including public safety, water and sewer, parks and recreation, planning and zoning, and code enforcement. The City of Payne City initially voted against consolidation but was dissolved by the Georgia General Assembly in 2015 and merged into Macon-Bibb County. Robert Reichert was the first mayor of the consolidated government (2014-2020), succeeded by Lester Miller in January 2020.
hypothetical_questions: What is a consolidated government?
What services does Macon-Bibb provide?
What happened to Payne City?
Who was the first mayor after consolidation?

content: The Macon-Bibb County Government Center is located at 700 Poplar Street, Macon, GA 31201. The main government phone number is (478) 751-7400, and the official website is maconbibb.us. Macon is the county seat of Bibb County, which is also the name of the consolidated government's legal entity for state-law purposes.
hypothetical_questions: Where is the Macon-Bibb government office?
What is the address of city hall?
How do I contact the government?
What is the county seat?

content: The Macon-Bibb County Commission holds regular meetings on the first and third Tuesdays of each month at 6:00 PM in the Commission Chambers at 700 Poplar Street. A Committee of the Whole meeting is held on the second Tuesday of each month at 9:00 AM. All meetings are open to the public, livestreamed on the county's Facebook page, and archived at maconbibb.tv.
hypothetical_questions: When does the commission meet?
When are commission meetings?
Where are commission meetings held?
Can I watch commission meetings online?

content: Meeting agendas and minutes for the Macon-Bibb County Commission are posted at maconbibbcoga.portal.civicclerk.com. Meeting videos are archived on YouTube and Vimeo at maconbibb.tv. All commission business except limited executive sessions is conducted in public under Georgia's Open Meetings Act.
hypothetical_questions: Where can I find commission agendas?
Are commission minutes public?
How do I see past commission meetings?

content: The official Macon-Bibb County government website is maconbibb.us. The Bibb County Board of Elections website is maconbibb.us/board-of-elections. The county also operates an open data portal called Macon Insights at maconinsights.maconbibb.us with public dashboards, maps, and data.
hypothetical_questions: What is the official Macon-Bibb website?
Where is the government website?
Is there an open data portal?

content: Macon-Bibb County uses SeeClickFix as its 311 system for reporting non-emergency issues such as potholes, code violations, and other service requests. The online services portal at maconbibb.us/online-services provides access to various government functions. A directory of department phone numbers is at maconbibb.us/helpful-phone-numbers.
hypothetical_questions: How do I report a pothole?
What is the 311 number for Macon?
How do I report a code violation?
How do I contact city services?

content: Bibb County is part of the Macon Judicial Circuit, which also includes Crawford and Peach Counties and is served by five Superior Court judges. The circuit's District Attorney's office can be reached at (478) 621-6427 with a website at maconjudicialcircuitda.com. Bibb County also falls within the U.S. District Court for the Middle District of Georgia and the 11th Circuit Court of Appeals.
hypothetical_questions: What judicial circuit is Macon in?
What courts serve Macon?
Who is the District Attorney?
How many Superior Court judges are there?

content: The Bibb County School District serves approximately 21,000 students across 35 or more schools, including elementary, middle, high, and specialty programs. The superintendent is Dr. Dan A. Sims, who has served since July 2022. The school board consists of eight members serving four-year terms, and the district's website is bcsdk12.net.
hypothetical_questions: How many schools are in Bibb County?
Who is the schools superintendent?
How big is the Bibb County School District?
How many school board members are there?

content: The Macon-Bibb County Tax Commissioner's office is located at 188 Third Street and can be reached at (478) 621-6500, with a website at maconbibbtax.us. The Board of Assessors website is bibbassessors.com, and the Planning and Zoning Commission website is mbpz.org. These offices handle property tax billing, property assessments, and land use decisions.
hypothetical_questions: How do I pay my property taxes?
Who is the tax commissioner?
Where is the tax office?
How do I contact the assessor?

content: The most important voting resources for Macon-Bibb County are the Georgia My Voter Page at mvp.sos.ga.gov for personalized sample ballots and polling locations, the Bibb County Board of Elections at maconbibb.us/board-of-elections or (478) 621-6622, and the Georgia Secretary of State's site at sos.ga.gov. Voters can register online at registertovote.sos.ga.gov and request absentee ballots at securemyabsenteeballot.sos.ga.gov. All of these resources are free.
hypothetical_questions: Where can I find voting information?
How do I access my voter information?
What are the best voting resources?
source: Georgia Secretary of State; Bibb County Board of Elections

content: Macon-Bibb County is divided between U.S. Congressional Districts 2 and 12 following the court-ordered 2023 redistricting. The county is also covered by Georgia State Senate Districts 18 and 26 and State House Districts 142, 143, 144, and 145. Voters can find their exact state and federal districts by entering their address at legis.ga.gov/find-my-legislator or mvp.sos.ga.gov.
hypothetical_questions: What congressional district am I in?
What state senate district is Macon in?
What state house district is Macon in?
How do I find my legislators?
source: Georgia General Assembly; Georgia Secretary of State
